div.div: · deck with one card slightly wider (long card) than others

Effect: You find a spectator's chosen card by touch or smell alone, eyes closed or while holding the deck behind your back. Method: prepare the deck with one 'locator' card trimmed ever-so-slightly wider (about 1mm) than the rest. When a card is selected and returned, control it to sit adjacent to the locator. Hold the deck behind your back or overhead and run your fingertips along the edge — the locator card stands out clearly by feel. The chosen card is right next to it. Dramatically 'sniff' each card as you approach the selection for theatrical effect.

Marked cards were not considered cheating in Victorian parlor magic — they were accepted performance tools. Professional gamblers, however, used much more subtle markings (pin pricks, ink shading) to cheat at Faro and Poker, and Victorian books on magic frequently warned readers about these criminal applications of the same principles.

A spectator names their card after the performer feels and 'smells' the cards one by one. The secret is a prepared card: one card is slightly wider than the others, so it can be located by touch even with eyes closed or in the dark. The performer uses this marked card to locate any selected card by controlling it near the prepared one.

svg.text: Source: The Magician's Own Book (1862) · Heritage Parlor
